title: Irish potato farls
subtitle: Ulster griddle bread from mashed potato and flour
excerpt: Eight small, soft potato griddle cakes made from floury mashed potato and a little plain flour, cooked in a dry pan. Ready in under 30 minutes once the potatoes are mashed. A staple of the Ulster fry, and equally good with butter and a fried egg.
sourceNotes: Ulster Scots griddle bread tradition documented in pre-1928 Northern Irish and Scottish household cookery manuscripts; potato farls appear in early-nineteenth-century accounts of Ulster domestic cooking and in the Farm and Home Cookery column of the Northern Whig (digitised, British Newspaper Archive). Cross-referenced with Scottish potato scone (tattie scone) methods of the same period, which follow near-identical proportions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, freeze in a single layer, then bag. Reheat in a dry pan from frozen for 3 to 4 minutes per side.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or triples easily. Cook in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mash the potatoes up to a day ahead and refrigerate. Mix and cook just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: No oven. Cook on a dry griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat. The pan is ready when a pinch of flour browns in 10 seconds.

glossaryTerms.definition: An Ulster Scots word for a flatbread divided into four triangular sections. The round of dough is cooked whole, then cut into quarters while still warm. The word comes from the Old Scots 'fardel', meaning a fourth part. Potato farls, soda farls, and wheaten farls all follow the same shape.

content.text: Potato farls are Ulster's griddle bread: soft, slightly floury rounds made almost entirely from mashed potato, cooked in a dry pan. The name comes from the Ulster Scots word for a quarter-cut flatbread: each round is cooked whole and cut into four

while still warm. Eight farls from one batch, ready in under 30 minutes once the potatoes are mashed. Use a floury variety such as Maris Piper or King Edward; waxy potatoes will not absorb the flour properly.

items.prepNote: floury variety, boiled and mashed while hot

content.text: Boil the potatoes in salted water until completely tender, 20 to 25 minutes. Drain and mash thoroughly while still hot; lumps will prevent the dough from rolling flat. Add the melted butter and salt, mix, then add the flour and bring together into a soft dough. It will be slightly sticky. Turn it out onto a lightly floured surface and knead briefly, about 1 minute, just until smooth.
Divide the dough in half. Roll each piece into a round about 20 cm across and 5 mm thick. Cut each round into four triangular quarters.

Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat with no oil. Cook the farls for 3 to 4 minutes per side, until pale brown patches appear and they feel dry to the touch. They should not colour deeply. Serve warm with butter, or let them cool and reheat in the pan to order.
